---
title: "Ordinary vs Universal Kriging: Why Stationarity Matters"
author: "Sheldon Downs"
date: "2026-07-10"
categories: [analysis, spatial statistics]
image: "image_80a04ccc.png"
---

## What is Kriging?

Kriging is a spatial interpolation technique that predicts values at unmeasured 
locations using nearby known values. It is the same technique oil and gas companies 
use to predict what lies underground between drill sites. In my project I applied 
it to NYC traffic sensors to predict traffic volume across the entire city.

But there are two versions of Kriging and picking the right one matters.

## Ordinary vs Universal Kriging

**Ordinary Kriging** assumes traffic volume has a roughly constant mean across 
the whole city. It just interpolates locally based on nearby sensors.

**Universal Kriging** accounts for an entire spatial trend in the data before 
interpolating. It is more complex but more accurate when the mean changes 
systematically across space.

The choice comes down to one question: is your data stationary?

## Testing for Stationarity

Stationarity means the mean and variance are stable across the study area. 
I tested this by dividing NYC into four quadrants and running a Kruskal-Wallis 
test to see if traffic volumes differed significantly between them.

I expected the data to be stationary. I was wrong.

## Results

| Time Period | H-Statistic | Result |
|---|---|---|
| AM Peak | 86.67 | Non-stationary |
| PM Peak | 43.77 | Non-stationary |
| Off Peak | 85.99 | Non-stationary |

Traffic volume drops systematically from northwest to southeast NYC. Manhattan 
and the Bronx have consistently higher volumes than the outer boroughs such as denser 
roads, more commercial activity, more traffic. That is a spatial trend, not 
random noise, and Ordinary Kriging would have ignored it entirely.

## The Fix

Switching to Universal Kriging with a regional linear drift term told the model 
to first capture the NW to SE gradient across the city, then interpolate the 
local variation around that trend. The result is a prediction surface that 
reflects both the city-wide pattern and the local sensor data.

## Conclusion

Testing for stationarity before fitting a Kriging model is not optional since it 
determines which version of Kriging is valid for your data. The result surprised 
me but it made the final prediction surface more honest and more accurate.
